items.q: Which type of muscle is under voluntary control?
items.why: Students think all muscles can be controlled if we try hard enough. Cardiac and smooth muscle are involuntary - you can't consciously beat your heart. Only skeletal muscle responds to conscious commands.

items.q: What sport is best suited to type I (slow-twitch) fibres?
items.why: Students confuse which fibres suit which event. Type I = slow, fatigue-resistant, aerobic (endurance). Type II = fast, powerful, anaerobic (sprint/power). Distance runners are type-I-dominant.

items.q: When you flex your elbow, which muscle is the agonist?
items.why: Students confuse agonist (the prime mover doing the action) with antagonist (the opposing muscle that relaxes). In elbow flexion, biceps is the agonist; triceps is the antagonist (and vice versa for extension).

items.q: Type IIx fibres are best suited to:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Brief maximal-power efforts. “Marathons” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Type I fibres. “Type IIx fibres” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a 100 m sprinter has 70% Type II fibres and a marathoner 25%, percentage-point difference is?
items.why: Exact answer: 45. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
